value.Title: Wind and Solar Energy Developers Should Review Preliminary Ruling Blocking Restrictions

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-K4C3-RWWG-D3VK-00000-00Wind and Solar Energy Developers Should Review Preliminary Ruling Blocking Restrictions2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:58ZLexisNexisWind and Solar Energy Developers Should Review Preliminary Ruling Blocking Restrictions

The views expressed in any and all content distributed by Newstex and its re-distributors (collectively, the "Newstex Authoritative Content") are solely those of the respective author(s) and not necessarily the views of Newstex or its re-distributors. Stories from such authors are provided "AS IS," with no warranties, and confer no rights. The material and information provided in Newstex Authoritative Content are for general information only and should not, in any respect, be relied on as professional advice. Newstex Authoritative Content is not "read and approved" before it is posted. Accordingly, neither Newstex nor its re-distributors make any claims, promises or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained therein or linked to from such content, nor do they take responsibility for any aspect of such content. The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

McGuireWoods LLP Heather Nixon Stevenson

May 14th, 2026 ( JD Supra - Delivered by Newstex )

Wind and solar energy developers with pending federal permits, rights-of-way or consultation requests should be assessing a recent federal court decision preliminarily blocking the Trump administration's sweeping restrictions on such projects. If the developers' plans fall within the scope of the injunction, they should take steps to advance their applications while the opportunity exists.

The situation is fluid, however. The administration has until mid-June to appeal the ruling, and, although apparently stalled now, Congress could weigh in with a bill significantly affecting the regulatory landscape.

The current and potential changes stem from a decision on April 21, 2026, by Chief Judge Denise J. Casper of the United States District Court for the District of Massachusetts who issued a preliminary injunction blocking the administration from enforcing five federal agency actions imposing sweeping restrictions on federal permitting for wind and solar energy projects. The ruling in RENEW Northeast v. U.S. Dept. of the Interior, Case No. 25-cv-13961 (D. Mass. Apr. 21, 2026), may allow substantial stalled renewable capacity to resume the federal permitting process - at least temporarily - without the secretary-level review requirements and capacity-density restrictions imposed since mid-2025.

Background

Beginning in 2025, the U.S. Department of the Interior (DOI) and the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (Corps) issued a series of orders and memoranda implementing White House executive orders targeting wind and solar development (collectively, the Actions):

DOI Review Procedures Memo (July 15, 2025)

The DOIrequired that three senior officials approve all discretionary actions related to wind and solar projects - covering at least 68 categories of permitting decisions - culminating in review by Interior Secretary Doug Burgum.

IPaC Ban (July 2025)

The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service barred wind and solar projects from using the Information for Planning and Consultation (IPaC) website - a key tool for streamlining endangered species review - until the DOI completed the Review Procedures.

DOI Land Order/Secretarial Order No. 3438 (Aug. 1, 2025)

The DOIreceived direction to approve only energy projects with the highest capacity density (nameplate capacity capacity factor ÷ total project acres), a metric that effectively disqualified most wind and solar projects in favor of nuclear, gas and coal.

Corps Memo (Sept. 18, 2025)

The Corps received direction to deprioritize Clean Water Act Section 404 and Rivers and Harbors Act Section 10 permits for wind and solar projects in favor of higher-capacity-density energy sources.

Zerzan M-Opinion (May 1, 2025)

The DOI's solicitor withdrew the prior administration's interpretation of the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act Section 8(p)(4) and reinstated a more restrictive standard requiring the DOI to prevent offshore activity causing more than de minimis interference with other outer continental shelf uses - effectively blocking new offshore wind construction and operation plan approvals and triggering re-evaluation of previously approved plans.

On Dec. 23, 2025, nine regional organizations representing renewable energy developers filed a complaint in the U.S. District Court of Massachusetts seeking a declaration that the Actions violated the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) and seeking temporary and permanent injunctions against their implementation. The plaintiff organizations include RENEW Northeast, Alliance for Clean Energy New York, Renewable Northwest, Southern Renewable Energy Association, Interwest Energy Alliance, Mid-Atlantic Renewable Energy Coalition Action, Clean Grid Alliance, Carolinas Clean Energy Business Association and Green Energy Consumers Alliance.

The Court's Analysis

One key to the court's opinion was its conclusion that the plaintiffs are likely to succeed on the merits of their claims that four of the five challenged actions are arbitrary and capricious in violation of the APA. Specifically, the court found that the DOI Review Procedures Memo, the Wind and Solar IPaC Ban, the Corps Memo and the Zerzan M-Opinion were likely arbitrary and capricious for several reasons. They included a lack of sufficient explanation or justification for each action and failure to point to distinctions between wind and solar projects and other types of projects warranting increased review or scrutiny.

The court found that the DOI failed to provide any justification for the DOI Review Procedures Memo beyond reliance on executive orders and secretarial orders. Following prior court decisions, the court ruled that even when following executive orders, an agency still must provide a reasoned basis for its actions.

Regarding the DOI Land Order's capacity density requirement, the court held that the plaintiffs would likely succeed on the argument that it is contrary to law - specifically, that the DOI Land Order's restriction on projects with lower capacity density contradicts statutory obligations, including the Federal Land Policy and Management Act's requirement to balance competing uses of federal land - but not that the policy is arbitrary and capricious.

Since the court found that the Actions were either arbitrary and capricious or contrary to law, it did not address the plaintiffs' other arguments.

The court also focused on irreparable harm to the plaintiffs, due to the likely lack of potential recovery against the government because of sovereign immunity. Industry groups presented evidence that the Actions jeopardized tens of gigawatts of renewable capacity, with significant downstream effects on investment, grid reliability and energy costs. The court found that the balance of equities and public interest favored the injunction, noting the public's interest in reliable, affordable energy and in ensuring that government agencies follow the law. While the agencies referenced the 'intrusive effect' of an injunction on agency operations, the court countered by expressly stating that the agency defendants have 'no interest in perpetuating unlawful agency practices that do not comply with the APA.'

Relief Granted

Consistent with the U.S. Supreme Court's recent decision in Trump v. CASA, Inc., 606 U.S. 831 (2025), the court declined to issue a universal nationwide injunction. Instead, the injunction applies only to the entity plaintiffs and - for all plaintiffs except the Green Energy Consumers Alliance, which relied on its own organizational standing rather than the standing of its members - to all members of each entity plaintiff.

It remains to be seen whether the agencies will apply the injunction broadly to all projects or only to those projects in which the developer can demonstrate membership in one of the plaintiff organizations. Notably, however, the IPaC tool already removed language prohibiting wind and solar projects from using the database, suggesting at least some immediate practical effect.

Post-Decision Congressional Developments

In the days following the court's decision, the ruling became a focal point in congressional proceedings. On April 29, 2026, Interior Secretary Burgum appeared before the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee for a hearing on the DOI's fiscal year 2027 budget request. Democratic senators used the hearing to press Burgum on whether the DOI would comply with the preliminary injunction and advance stalled renewable energy permits. Although Burgum indicated that he disagreed with the court's ruling, when pressed, he stated that he was willing to process solar and wind permits.

Practical Implications for Renewable Energy Developers

Agency implementation uncertainty

Federal field offices may be slow to implement the injunction without guidance from Washington, particularly given Burgum's stated disagreement with the ruling. Developers should proactively notify relevant agencies of the injunction, document all communications and obtain written confirmation of their membership status in covered organizations.

Preliminary and subject to appeal

Because this is a preliminary injunction, not a final judgment, the government may appeal and may seek an emergency stay from the U.S. Court of Appeals for the First Circuit or the Supreme Court. The defendants have 60 days to appeal the preliminary injunction order, so some developers may use this window to advance projects rather than assume the ruling is permanent.

Limitations

The injunction does not address the separate Wind Memo issued by the White House on Jan. 20, 2025, which directed federal agencies to suspend issuing all new permits, leases and other authorizations needed to develop wind energy projects. That directive is the subject of separate litigation in State of New York v. Trump, currently on appeal before the First Circuit. The RENEW Northeast opinion relies heavily on the earlier New York v. Trump opinion issued by Judge Patti B. Saris in December 2025, and the First Circuit's decision on appeal may affect the durability of the RENEW Northeast ruling.

Congressional outlook

The April 29 Senate hearing signals that bipartisan permitting reform legislation - which is important to both renewable energy and fossil fuel developers - may remain stalled unless the administration demonstrates a willingness to process wind and solar permits. Developers should monitor these legislative developments closely, as any permitting reform bill could significantly affect the regulatory landscape for energy projects on federal lands and waters.

value.Title: Hiltzik: Trump's 'weird war' on wind power will jeopardize our energy future and cost Americans billions

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK2-XN03-RYJ9-220X-00000-00Hiltzik: Trump's 'weird war' on wind power will jeopardize our energy future and cost Americans billions2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:58ZLexisNexisHiltzik: Trump's 'weird war' on wind power will jeopardize our energy future and cost Americans billionsMichael Hiltzik

Picking the wildest fantasy promoted by President Trump as a basis for public policy is increasingly challenging - is it his yarn about schoolchildren being secretly abducted from their classrooms and given sex-changing operations? The notion that the vaccines given to children are like "a vat, like a big glass, of stuff pumped into their bodies?"

Here's one that has disrupted the economics of renewable energy generation and will cost Americans billions of dollars: It's Trump's "completely weird war on wind power in the United States," based on a sheaf of "fact-free arguments."

That judgment comes from Steven Cohen, a climate policy expert at Columbia University, who points out that wind already accounts for 10.5% of U.S. energy generation, that it's destined to continue growing - and that most of it is generated today in red states such as Texas, Oklahoma, Iowa and Kansas.

There is no question that Trump's weird war against wind is full blown. On the day of his second inauguration, he issued an executive order shutting down all new permits for offshore wind farms and ordered the Interior Department to review existing permits.

A federal judge in Massachusettsblocked the executive order in December, and his orders suspending work on existing offshore wind projects have been halted by other federal judges. The Trump administration has blocked or delayed as many as 165 wind projects on private land, citing "national security" concerns, according to the American Clean Power Assn.

Most recently, Trump has reached agreements with offshore wind firms in which the government will pay them a combined $2 billion to abandon their U.S. projects.

At some level, this crusade resembles Trump's misguided effort to revive the American coal industry, which is on the glide path to inevitable extinction. In that case, Trump is waging an explicitly partisan and ideological battle. "We're ending Joe Biden's war on beautiful, clean coal," he declared last April.

Trump's anti-wind program is part of his campaign to dismantle U.S. renewables policy because of its roots in the Biden administration.

Additionally, multiple commentators conjecture that his hostility to wind originated in 2011, when he groused that an offshore wind farm would be visible from one of his golf courses in Scotland. He sued to thwart the "ugly" project, and lost.

But Trump has mustered other arguments against wind, on- and offshore, none of which holds water.

During a cabinet meeting in July 2025, he called wind "a very expensive form of energy." In fact, on average it's cheaper than natural gas, coal and nuclear generation. Perhaps more important, the cost has been coming down sharply as technology improves and the sector reaches critical mass: falling to eight cents from 21 cents per kilowatt-hour from 2010 to 2024 for offshore projects, and to 3.4 cents from 11.3 cents for land-based wind farms over the same period.

Trump blamed wind turbines for mass killing whales and birds. Neither assertion is correct.

The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, a federal agency, says "there are no known links between large whale deaths and ongoing offshore wind activities."

The Audubon Society reported in January that although wind turbines can present hazards to birds, "developers can effectively manage these risks without significantly increasing project costs." The biggest risks to birds come from the climate: "Two-thirds of North American birds are at increasing risk of extinction from global temperature rise," the society reported - a threat that wind power can ameliorate.

Trump spokeswoman Taylor Rogers didn't respond to my questions about the derivation of his anti-wind stance, but told me by email only that "President Trump has been clear: hard-earned taxpayer dollars shouldn't be wasted on unreliable and costly wind farms that pose serious threats to our national security. Instead, we should be strengthening and expanding our infrastructure that produces reliable, affordable, and secure energy like natural gas plants."

That brings us to the recent deals with offshore wind developers. The largest single deal, signed in March, was with the French firm TotalEnergies, which is to receive approximately $1 billion from the federal government to abandon all of its U.S. offshore wind projects and invest instead in oil and gas projects, including a liquefied natural gas export facility in Texas.

In his March 23 announcement of the deal, Interior Secretary Doug Burgum called offshore wind "one of the most expensive, unreliable, environmentally disruptive, and subsidy-dependent schemes ever forced on American ratepayers and taxpayers."

This is what Huck Finn would call a "stretcher," given the decades of subsidies spooned out to the oil and gas industry, reaching more than $30 billion a year in federal and state tax credits, indulgent regulation of pollution and low-cost access to federal lands. Indeed, the investment firm Lazard recently reported that renewables, including wind, are a cost-competitive form of generation even without subsidies. (Lazard's calculation is of the "levelized cost of energy," meaning the average cost over a generating plant's lifetime.)

TotalEnergies fell into lockstep with the Interior Department in its own announcement, explaining its willingness to renounce U.S. offshore wind power because "offshore wind developments in the United States, unlike those in Europe, are costly," echoing the agency's position that "the development of offshore wind projects is not in the country's interest." Never mind that one factor that makes U.S. offshore wind development costly compared with Europe is the Trump administration's opposition.

The government subsequently reached an agreement to pay the French company Ocean Winds $885 million to walk away from two offshore wind projects, including one in the waters off California. Ocean Winds described the deal as one driven chiefly by economics, but hinted at pressure from the White House.

"We welcome the opportunity to engage constructively with the administration on this agreement and acknowledge the clarity they have provided with this decision and deal," Michael Brown, the chief executive of Ocean Winds North America, said when the deal was announced last month. "Our priority remains disciplined capital allocation and delivering reliable energy solutions that create long-term value for ratepayers, partners, and shareholders."

The TotalEnergies deal, which the government has described as a "refund" of money the firm paid for its offshore leades, raised the hackles of congressional Democrats, who assert that it violates the law and constitution in multiple ways.

"We will hold you accountable for this billion-dollar ripoff," Reps. Jamie Raskin (D-Md.), ranking member of the House Judiciary Committee and Jared Huffman (D-San Rafael), ranking member of the House Committee on Natural Resources, warned TotalEnergies CEO Patrick Pouyanné in an April 29 letter.

Among other infirmities Raskin and Huffman alleged, the government's national security rationale for canceling offshore wind leases looks "fabricated"; the payout violates the statutory formula for compensation for canceled leases; the money is to come from a fund designed only to pay court-ordered judgments and settlements of lawsuits, which don't exist in this case; and includes a provision preventing the deal from being reviewed by a court.

The last of those provisions would have to be authorized by Congress, the letter states, asking for documents and a response from the company by Wednesday. Committee spokespersons weren't available to say whether they received a response from TotalEnergies, and the company didn't respond to my request for comment. I received no response from the Department of the Interior.

The California Energy Commission has opened an investigation into the Ocean Winds deal.

"The Trump Administration is recklessly spending billions of taxpayer dollars on backroom deals that would turn back the clock on innovation" CEC Chair David Hochschild said. "Taxpayer dollars should be used to build a sustainable energy future, not to pay to make projects disappear."

What's especially wasteful about Trump's crusade against wind power is that it's almost certain to be time-limited.

It's hardly debatable that renewables such as solar and wind will be our principal sources of energy in the future; holding back the clock achieves nothing but injecting uncertainty into investment decisions that need to be made now, at a time when the price of oil is on the upswing thanks to Trump's Iran adventure and Europe and China are racing to transition away from fossil fuels, while the U.S. remains becalmed by ideology.

"In the long run, fossil fuels will be used for petrochemicals and not for burning," Cohen told me. "Fifty years from now, people are going to be amazed that we burned these rare, useful hydrocarbons for fuel, when the sun was just sitting up there providing an essentially infinite source of energy."

This story originally appeared in Los Angeles Times.

SECTION 3 CORPORATE PROFILE

3.1 Activities

EnerNOC Inc., a pioneer in energy intelligence and demand response solutions, operates primarily in the energy management sector, with a significant presence in markets like Germany. The company's core business focuses on providing software and services that enable commercial and industrial clients to optimize energy consumption, reduce operational costs, and enhance grid reliability through demand response programs and real-time data analytics. By leveraging advanced technologies, EnerNOC helps organizations integrate renewable energy sources and improve efficiency. The fastest-growing segments include its digital platforms for energy monitoring and predictive analytics, fueled by the rising demand for sustainable practices and regulatory incentives in Europe. This expansion is supported by partnerships that address the challenges of decentralized energy systems, positioning EnerNOC as a key player in the transition to a greener economy, particularly in Germany's dynamic renewable energy landscape. It is Germany's 18th largest Electric company by market capitalisation.

May 11th, 2026 ( Ticker Report - Delivered by Newstex )

Algonquin Power & Utilities (NYSE:AQN) reported lower first-quarter 2026 earnings than a year earlier, while management said the company continued to make progress on its plan to become a premier pure-play regulated utility through regulatory settlements, operational improvements and balance sheet actions.

Chief Executive Officer Rod West said the quarter represented a solid start for Algonquin and showed progress on the companys Path to Premier, a strategy focused on consistent execution, constructive regulatory outcomes and disciplined financial and operational management.

In short, were advancing toward our goal of becoming a premier pure-play regulated utility, West said on the earnings call.

First-quarter earnings decline from prior year

Chief Financial Officer Rob Stefani said Algonquin reported first-quarter GAAP net earnings of $83.1 million, compared with $92.8 million in the same period of 2025. Adjusted net earnings were $99.6 million, down from $109 million a year earlier.

Adjusted net earnings per share attributable to common shareholders were $0.13, down one cent from the prior-year quarter. Stefani said the decline reflected the non-recurrence of favorable depreciation and tax adjustments recorded in the first quarter of 2025, as well as slightly unfavorable weather compared with the prior-year period. Those factors were almost entirely offset by a favorable retroactive adjustment tied to the resolution of the CalPeco Electric general rate case, he said.

Stefani said CalPeco was the largest driver of year-over-year results. The California Public Utilities Commission approved a settlement that increased annualized rates by $48.6 million and included $60.7 million in retroactive revenues to Jan. 1, 2025. The benefit was partially offset by $28.5 million in higher wildfire insurance expenses recovered in rates, including $22.7 million of retroactive insurance expenses to the first quarter of 2025.

Excluding the retroactive adjustment, higher operating expenses were driven by $3.8 million in Gas Safety Excellence costs, along with higher labor, benefits and property taxes across Algonquins gas systems. Depreciation rose $12.9 million, mainly because two favorable depreciation deferrals recorded in the first quarter of 2025 did not recur. Tax expense increased $9 million, primarily due to the non-recurrence of a favorable 2025 tax adjustment related to the companys hydro group following the January 2025 sale of its renewable energy business.

Regulatory settlements advance across several jurisdictions

West highlighted several regulatory developments during the quarter, including approvals and agreements involving New England Gas, CalPeco Electric, The Empire District Electric Co. and ESSAL, the companys Chilean water utility.

In Massachusetts, the Department of Public Utilities approved Algonquins New England Natural Gas settlement agreement on March 27, providing for a $45.3 million revenue adjustment. In California, regulators approved the CalPeco Electric settlement on March 19, with one change related to a proposed adjustment to the fixed charge for residential customers, which will remain unchanged.

For Empire Electric Missouri, West said the company is awaiting commission review of customer performance data. Under an approved settlement, Algonquin must demonstrate three consecutive months of customer metric performance before implementing $97 million in annualized revenues. The company has submitted three monthly filings and identified limited deviations in each month, which West said were consistent with the settlement provisions. He said Algonquin expects the commission to rule by midyear.

The company is also working with stakeholders on a potential additional $13 million of annual revenues tied to further performance requirements beginning in the second half of 2026.

Other pending matters include a settlement agreement and formula rate plan decision at Liberty Utilities Litchfield Park Water & Sewer business in Arizona, where Algonquin has requested a decision by August. In California, the company continues to pursue recovery of approximately $77 million in wildfire-related costs through its WEMA proceeding, primarily related to claim settlements above insurance coverage, legal costs and financing costs from the 2020 Mountain View Fire.

Algonquin also has pending matters at Park Water and Apple Valley in California and a Kansas rate case at Empire Electric seeking a $15.8 million base rate change. West said ESSAL reached an agreement with Chilean regulators that includes a $4 million rate adjustment expected in the second half of the year.

Company emphasizes operational and customer improvements

West said Algonquin has begun rolling out updated procedures and training materials for damage prevention and leak response in its gas operations. The company has also implemented independent quality assurance and quality control reviews of line locating activities.

On the customer side, West said Algonquin improved billing accuracy in Missouri versus prior periods and improved communications to customers in the moments that matter to them.

West said the companys regulatory strategy is centered on earlier engagement with stakeholders, more pragmatic filings and improved accuracy. Were getting back to regulatory basics to earn our rights to grow, he said.

Balance sheet, refinancing and possible redomicile

Stefani said Algonquins balance sheet remains in a position of strength, noting that Fitch recently reaffirmed the companys credit ratings in the BBB range. Algonquin also remains rated BBB by S&P and Baa2 by Moodys.

The company expects to refinance senior unsecured notes due in June by raising approximately $1.15 billion at LUCO through a 144A bond issuance. Stefani said Algonquin also recently put in place a $1.15 billion delayed-draw credit facility to support the refinancing if needed.

West said Algonquin continues to explore tax optimization strategies, including a potential redomicile, but no definitive decision has been made. Any move would require approval from the board, shareholders and other stakeholders. The company has requested a private letter ruling from the Internal Revenue Service to confirm tax treatment, a process West said typically takes six to nine months.

In response to an analyst question, Stefani said the company is asking the IRS to assess the tax implications of legally redomiciling into the United States, including the potential tax consequences.

CalPeco power supply and capital spending discussed in Q&A

During the question-and-answer session, RBC Capital Markets analyst Nelson Ng asked about CalPeco Electrics future power supply, citing reports that NV Energy would no longer supply electricity to CalPeco starting in May 2027. Stefani said Algonquin is launching a competitive search for new energy partners and has filed a request with the CPUC to begin that process. Formal bidding is expected to begin later this summer, with preferred selection in the winter of 2026-2027 and new power supply agreements expected by spring 2027, subject to CPUC approval.

Asked whether CalPeco may add generation of its own, Stefani said the company is focused on the competitive search for energy supply partners.

Ng also asked about first-quarter capital expenditures of approximately $88 million compared with full-year guidance of about $800 million. West said the timing reflected seasonality and project timing, and that Algonquin still expects to meet its capital plan.

West closed the call by saying the company had extended its 2025 momentum into the first quarter and remains confident in its plan. He also thanked employees for their contributions to the companys progress.

About Algonquin Power & Utilities (NYSE:AQN)

Algonquin Power & Utilities Corp (NYSE: AQN) is a diversified generation, transmission and distribution utility company headquartered in Oakville, Ontario. Established in 1988, the firm operates through two primary business segments: Regulated Utilities and Renewable Energy. Its Regulated Utilities segment comprises electric, natural gas and water distribution networks serving residential, commercial and industrial customers across North America, while its Renewable Energy portfolio includes hydroelectric, solar, wind and thermal generation facilities.

The company's renewable energy assets span multiple jurisdictions in Canada and the United States, reflecting its strategy to expand clean power capacity in regions with supportive regulatory frameworks.

value.Title: Fmr. Ogun commissioner, Adebajo joins FCMB Group

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ8-2B73-S7H1-G3RY-00000-00Fmr. Ogun commissioner, Adebajo joins FCMB Group2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:57ZLexisNexisFmr. Ogun commissioner, Adebajo joins FCMB Group

A former commissioner for Agriculture in OgunAdepejuAdebajo has joined FCMB Group Plc as an Independent Non-Executive Director to its Board, following the approval of the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN).

Adebajo who has served in various capacities is expected to bring her over 30 years of experience across industry, renewable energy, agriculture, finance, and consulting to the FCMB Group Board.

In a statement, it highlighted her specialisation in business transformation and turnarounds, with a strong record in strategy development, team leadership, and ESG implementation for clients in Sub-Saharan Africa and Europe.

It added that her background combines technical expertise and business insight, with degrees from Harvard Business School and Imperial College London, and further studies in Sustainability management.

'She has led teams in a range of sectors, including as CEO Cement at Lafarge Africa, UTC Nigeria, Mouka Limited and Lumos Nigeria, and AGM at UBA Plc. Earlier in her career, she worked with Boston Consulting Group,

supporting clients in London, Europe and the USA on business transformation and Citibank London.

'Adebajo also founded Pinky Blue Limited, the exclusive franchisee of Mothercare UK in Nigeria, and served as Commissioner for Agriculture in Ogun State from 2017 to 2019,' the statement reads.

It described her passion for climate and sustainability, saying she founded the Climate Governance Initiative Nigeria and served on the World Economic Forum's Global Future Council on Climate and Nature Governance.

'Her leadership has been recognised by Cranfield University, LinkedIn, CNBC Africa and Harvard Business School,' the statement added.

Adebajo has completed executive training at leading institutions, including the Advanced Management Programme at INSEAD, the Prince of Wales's Business and Sustainability Programme at the University of Cambridge, AI for Business Strategy at Massachusetts Institute of Technology Sloan School and the Citibank Management Trainee Programme in London and New York.

She currently serves as Chair of the Board of the Lagos State Employment Trust Fund and sits on the boards of Ibstock Plc, a UK FTSE 250 manufacturing company, Wolseley Limited, a UK private equity-owned distribution company and Husk Power Systems, a Distributed Energy Resources company providing power to communities in the Global South. She is Transformation Lead for Etex Group in Nigeria (Nigerite and Emenite).

She remains committed to increasing employment and employability, advancing the energy transition, sustainability, and mentoring women as an EMCC Qualified coach.

The Board of Directors welcomes Adebajo and believes her wealth of experience in business transformation, strategy development, operational excellence, and ESG implementation will strengthen FCMB Group's leadership and

support its continued growth in the Nigerian market.

Her appointment reflects the Group's ongoing commitment to maintaining a robust governance framework and building a leadership team that supports innovation, inclusivity, and responsible practices.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHS-S813-S8K8-R2SN-00000-00SUFFOLK CONSTRUCTION - SECTOR UPDATES FROM PEERS2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:58ZLexisNexisSTOCKSUFFOLK CONSTRUCTION - SECTOR UPDATES FROM PEERS

DASHBOARD: SUFFOLK CONSTRUCTION

PermID5000317077Websitehttp://www.suffolk.com/IndustryConstruction & Engineering

ACTIVITIES:

Suffolk Construction, founded in 1982 by John Fish in Boston, Massachusetts, is a leading U.S. general contractor. With $5.5 billion in revenue and 2,500 employees, Suffolk delivers complex projects in healthcare, education, and commercial sectors. Notable works include Bostons Millennium Tower and Miamis Brickell City Centre. Employing advanced technologies like BIM and virtual design, Suffolk enhances project efficiency and safety. The company operates 10 offices nationwide, with a strong Northeast presence. Suffolks commitment to sustainability includes LEED-certified projects and eco-friendly practices. Its employee-centric culture, with robust training and diversity initiatives, earned it a spot on Fortunes Best Workplaces list. Suffolk continues to innovate, focusing on smart buildings and urban development.

+ SUFFOLK CONSTRUCTION [3 News That Matters]

+ SUFFOLK CONSTRUCTION'S LANE REOPENING ON ROUTE 58 SIGNALS PROGRESS

SUFFOLK, Va. — Motorists on U.S. Route 58 near downtown Suffolk will soon see relief as the Virginia Department of Transportation (VDOT) begins removing a long-term lane closure starting as early as Monday night. In a significant move to bolster local economic development, the Jefferson County Commission has approved a jobs grant for Brasfield & Gorrie, a prominent construction firm headquartered in Birmingham. On 25th March 2026, the Commission unanimously passed the resolution, which supports the company’s expansion plans that include a new office tower, a parking deck expansion, a training and event center, and a wellness center.Commission President Jimmie Stephens emphasized the importance of Brasfield & Gorrie to the region, noting that the company has been a stalwart presence in Birmingham since its inception. "They’ve chosen to remain here although they are a worldwide company," he remarked, highlighting the firm's commitment to the local economy.The expansion represents a substantial investment of $64,584,500 from Brasfield & Gorrie, with $2,884,500 allocated for land acquisition, $56,100,000 designated for construction, and $5,600,000 earmarked for equipment. This development is expected to create 80 new jobs, each offering an hourly wage of $39.51. The county’s jobs grant, capped at $240,000, is designed to incentivize this growth.In return for its investment, Jefferson County anticipates a return of $8,000 to the general fund and $1,011,773 to the education fund, illustrating the potential benefits of supporting local businesses like Brasfield & Gorrie. Stephens expressed gratitude for the confidence the company has shown in the county, vowing to maintain a strong partnership moving forward.As the economic landscape continues to evolve, the expansion of Brasfield & Gorrie underscores the importance of local support for sustainable growth. The firm’s decision to invest in Birmingham not only strengthens its operational base but also contributes positively to the broader community.

+ BRASFIELD & GORRIE UNVEILS $64.5 MILLION EXPANSION PLANS FOR BIRMINGHAM HQ

Brasfield & Gorrie, one of Birmingham’s largest construction firms, is planning a major expansion at its national headquarters, a move company leaders say reflects continued growth and a long-term commitment to the city. The company presented plans to the Birmingham City Council committee on 10th March 2026 for a new office building and parking expansion at its headquarters campus along 7th Avenue South.The proposal outlines a project expected to bring millions of dollars in investment and dozens of new jobs to the city. Brasfield & Gorrie plans to construct a new four-story office building totaling roughly 80,000 square feet. According to company representatives, the first floor will serve as an event and training center designed to host gatherings and professional development opportunities, featuring a large banquet room, an auditorium, and several meeting and training rooms.The remaining three floors are planned for office space that will support Brasfield & Gorrie’s expanding national operations. Company leaders say the additional space will allow them to accommodate new employees while leaving room for future growth. In addition to the new building, Brasfield & Gorrie plans to add a fourth level to its existing parking deck, creating approximately 125 additional parking spaces for employees and visitors.As part of the expansion, Brasfield & Gorrie plans to create 80 new full-time jobs headquartered in Birmingham, with an expected average salary of about $82,000. During the council committee discussion, company leaders emphasized that the new roles will help support the firm’s operations across the country while keeping its headquarters firmly rooted in Birmingham. They stated, 'It will be jobs headquartered here in Birmingham to support our field and our operations. We’re really growing, and we’re committed to staying in Birmingham.'The project represents an estimated $64.5 million investment. Under the proposed agreement, the City of Birmingham would provide financial incentives of up to $80,000 to support the project. The proposal received approval from the council committee and will now move to the full Birmingham City Council for final consideration. If approved, company leaders say construction on the expansion could be completed by late 2027.

+ BRASFIELD & GORRIE RECEIVES $12M TAX INCENTIVE FOR JEFFERSON COUNTY PROJECT

Birmingham-based construction firm Brasfield & Gorrie has secured a substantial economic incentive package from state officials, aimed at bolstering its $65 million project in Jefferson County. On 20th February 2026, the Alabama Department of Commerce approved nearly $12 million in tax credits for the company, which includes $9.69 million in investment tax credits and $2.24 million in jobs tax credits.The incentives are part of a strategic initiative to promote economic development in the region and are expected to play a significant role in the execution of Brasfield & Gorrie's ambitious project. This funding will not only support the immediate development but also contribute to job creation and local investment, which aligns with the state's broader economic goals.Brasfield & Gorrie, a privately held firm recognized for its extensive portfolio in construction and project management, has been a key player in Alabama's construction landscape. The approval of these tax credits underscores the state's commitment to fostering growth within its local businesses, particularly in the construction sector, which has faced challenges amid fluctuating economic conditions.The $64.6 million project will likely enhance Brasfield & Gorrie's operational capabilities and expand its service offerings, further solidifying its market position. As the company moves forward with the development, stakeholders will be keenly observing how these incentives translate into tangible benefits for both Brasfield & Gorrie and the Jefferson County community.In conclusion, the Alabama Department of Commerce's support through this tax incentive package marks a significant milestone for Brasfield & Gorrie, reinforcing its role in driving economic progress in the region. The anticipated outcomes of this project will not only reflect on the company's growth trajectory but also on the overall economic vitality of Birmingham and its surrounding areas.

+ JE DUNN CONSTRUCTION GROUP [3 News That Matters]

+ JE DUNN CONSTRUCTION UNVEILS FORM OFF-SITE SOLUTIONS TO ENHANCE MANUFACTURING CAPABILITIES

JE Dunn Construction has launched its new off-site manufacturing initiative, Form Off-Site Solutions(TradeMark), designed to specialize in Design for Manufacturing (DfM) for complex construction components. This strategic move comes as the company seeks to capitalize on its century-long legacy in Kansas City and beyond. The introduction of Form Off-Site Solutions is positioned as a response to the growing demand for advanced manufacturing capabilities, especially in a rapidly evolving construction landscape. The launch date of this initiative is 6th April 2026.Led by Off-Site Manufacturing Director Nick Effenheim, Form Off-Site Solutions brings together skilled craftspeople, advanced automation, and innovative strategies to design and produce specialized components for a diverse array of stakeholders, including owners, general contractors, trade partners, and original equipment manufacturers. The offerings are categorized into four distinct product segments: metal assemblies, wood assemblies, building skin assemblies, and multi-trade assemblies, ensuring that various market needs are met with tailored solutions.The end-to-end service model of Form Off-Site Solutions addresses the multifaceted challenges inherent in construction projects. By providing capabilities that range from estimating and design to manufacturing engineering, project management, and production, JE Dunn Construction aims to deliver improved installation efficiency, enhanced project safety and quality, and maximized cost certainty. This comprehensive approach is intended to alleviate labor shortages and accelerate project timelines, critical factors in today's construction environment.Effenheim emphasizes the collaborative nature of Form's operations, stating, "Anything Form designs, our people can build. Our ability to collaborate with project teams and focus on getting their ideas, concepts, and design into a manufactured environment is something that sets us apart." This commitment to collaboration and innovation is echoed by JE Dunn President and CEO Gordon Lansford, who notes that the development of Form Off-Site Solutions represents a significant advancement in the company's ongoing quest to drive innovation for its clients.With production facilities located in Kansas City and Nashville, Form Off-Site Solutions is poised to serve clients on a national scale. The company's focus on innovation and strategic thinking is a cornerstone of its operations, aimed at enhancing project safety and quality while also addressing labor shortages in the industry. As JE Dunn Construction continues to push the boundaries of off-site manufacturing, the launch of Form Off-Site Solutions marks a pivotal step in its mission to deliver projects with greater efficiency and certainty.

+ JE DUNN CONSTRUCTION CELEBRATES TOPPING-OUT MILESTONE FOR WORKFORCE CENTER

KANSAS CITY, Mo. — Construction has reached a key milestone on the Northland Workforce Development Center, as JE Dunn Construction and project partners recently celebrated the topping out of the new facility. The event, held on 19th March 2026, marked the placement of the final structural steel beam for the project, signaling significant progress on the 145,000-square-foot, two-story building designed to expand access to career and technical education across the Northland region.Prior to the beam’s installation, attendees participated in a traditional beam-signing ceremony, adding their names and messages to a steel beam bearing project and partner logos. This moment underscored the collaboration among the Platte County R-3 School District, partner school districts, project stakeholders, and the broader community in advancing a shared workforce development vision. Brad Hokanson, Project Executive at JE Dunn Construction, emphasized the importance of this collaboration, stating, 'This project is about creating opportunities and pathways for the next generation of skilled professionals in our region.'The Northland Workforce Development Center is designed to support programs in skilled trades, manufacturing, health sciences, and human services. Once complete, the facility will expand the capacity of Northland Career Center and its partners, serving both high school students and adult learners seeking industry-aligned training and credentials. Planned program spaces include flexible labs and classrooms, hands-on training environments, and technology-rich areas that reflect real-world workplace conditions.Located in the Northland region of Kansas City, the project is progressing on schedule, with substantial completion targeted in 2027 and a summer opening anticipated following the structural milestone. Brian Noller, Executive Director of Business Services and NWDC Campaign Lead at Platte County School District, highlighted the collaborative efforts, stating, 'With support from the State of Missouri, local municipalities, businesses and industries, individual investors, and community foundations, we are making a long-term investment in the success of our students and the future of our region.'JE Dunn is serving as construction manager for the project, working in partnership with the school district and design firm Clark & Enersen. The project team has emphasized safety, schedule adherence, and coordination throughout construction, while maintaining communication with stakeholders and minimizing disruption to nearby facilities. The Northland Workforce Development Center represents a regional investment in workforce readiness, aligning educational infrastructure with evolving industry needs and supporting long-term economic development in the Kansas City area.

+ CHRIS CHELETTE REJOINS JE DUNN TO BOLSTER HEALTHCARE SECTOR

JE Dunn Construction has announced the return of Chris Chelette, who will serve as the Senior Vice President – Healthcare. Chelette's reappointment comes after he spent nine years as the Senior Vice President of Facilities Services at Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta. This announcement was made on 13th June 2025.During his previous 17-year tenure with JE Dunn, Chelette played a crucial role in expanding the company's healthcare business unit. His extensive experience includes overseeing numerous high-profile projects across the United States, particularly within the non-profit and for-profit healthcare sectors. His return to JE Dunn is seen as a strategic move to enhance the company's healthcare presence in the southeastern region.In his new role, Chelette will be responsible for providing strategic leadership and operational oversight for healthcare construction projects throughout the southeast. Jeff Fuller, President of Healthcare at JE Dunn, expressed enthusiasm about Chelette's return, noting his visionary leadership and impactful experience in the healthcare field. Fuller emphasized that Chelette brings a national perspective and a proven track record that will benefit JE Dunn's future endeavors in healthcare construction.Throughout his career, Chelette has been instrumental in managing significant projects, such as the development of the North Druid Hills campus for Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta. This project, which totaled over $2 billion, featured the Center for Advanced Pediatrics, along with a state-of-the-art 446-bed replacement hospital that opened last year. Such accomplishments highlight Chelette's capability to manage complex healthcare construction initiatives effectively.As JE Dunn continues to invest in its healthcare construction division, Chelette's return is expected to drive growth and innovation in the company's project portfolio. The organization aims to leverage his expertise to enhance its service offerings and solidify its position as a leader in the healthcare construction industry.

+ GILBANE [3 News That Matters]

+ GILBANE DEVELOPMENT ELEVATES ANDREAS VLAHAKIS TO LEAD CAPITAL MARKETS

Gilbane Development has appointed Andreas Vlahakis as head of capital markets, a move aimed at strengthening the firm’s investment strategies. As of 28th April 2026, Vlahakis will operate from the company’s New York City office, bringing with him a wealth of experience in real estate capital markets across various asset classes, particularly in ground-up multifamily and student housing investments.In his new role at Gilbane Development, Vlahakis will be responsible for refining and executing the firm's funding strategy. Reporting directly to James Patchett, president and CEO, he will play a pivotal role in setting the capital strategy, making crucial investment decisions, and developing strong relationships with funding and equity partners. His expertise will also extend to leading transaction structuring and negotiations, positioning Gilbane to capitalize on emerging market opportunities.Before joining Gilbane Development, Vlahakis served as managing director for capital markets at Thor Equities, where he was instrumental in leading equity-raising efforts for the firm's various projects and partnerships. His prior experience also includes a role as senior vice president and chief of staff at Thor Equities, further underscoring his capability in navigating complex financial landscapes. Vlahakis's extensive background equips him with the insight necessary to enhance Gilbane's standing within the competitive real estate sector.Gilbane Development, known for its commitment to quality and innovation in real estate, is strategically positioning itself for growth with this significant leadership change. By appointing Vlahakis, the firm aims to leverage his expertise to enhance its capital strategies and investment portfolio, particularly in sectors that align with evolving market demands. Stakeholders and investors will be keenly observing how Vlahakis's leadership will influence Gilbane’s future endeavors.As the real estate market continues to evolve, the importance of strong leadership in capital markets becomes increasingly evident. Gilbane Development's decision to bring on Andreas Vlahakis reflects a proactive approach to securing its capital needs and driving forward its ambitious growth plans. Investors will likely be curious to see how Vlahakis's experience will translate into tangible results for the firm.

+ WKU ANNOUNCES GILBANE PARTNERSHIP, PAVING THE WAY FOR NEW DEVELOPMENTS

The Western Kentucky University (WKU) Student Government Association (SGA) has officially announced the approval of WKU's public-private partnership with Gilbane. This significant development indicates the beginning of a collaborative effort aimed at enhancing student facilities and resources. As part of the partnership, which was formally approved by WKU’s Board of Regents on 11th December 2025, Gilbane will design and develop a new residence hall, intended to replace the outdated Douglas Keen and Hugh Poland Halls.Following the recent approval from the Kentucky Legislature on 6th April 2026, WKU is poised to commence construction on the new residence halls. SGA Senator Jakob Barker confirmed that the partnership with Gilbane is now fully authorized, stating, "WKU is now in the clear to start building the new residence halls." This development not only addresses housing needs but also reflects WKU's commitment to improving the overall student experience.In addition to the housing initiative, the SGA has passed several legislative measures aimed at enhancing student resources. Among these is Resolution 7-26-S, which supports the installation of a crosswalk at a crucial intersection near student housing. The resolution highlights the safety concerns of students who currently navigate busy streets to access essential facilities, advocating for collaboration with the Kentucky Department of Transportation for this project.Further addressing financial constraints faced by students, Bill 17-26-S was also approved, establishing the Textbooks for Toppers reimbursement program. This initiative will provide $50 reimbursements for students purchasing required course materials, a move that reflects the SGA's recognition of the financial challenges many students encounter. Additionally, other bills passed will provide essential academic supplies, such as Scantrons and lab goggles, ensuring that students have access to necessary materials without added financial burden.As WKU moves forward with its partnership with Gilbane and implements these new funding initiatives, the SGA's commitment to improving the student experience is clear. The collaboration with Gilbane not only aims to enhance student housing but also signifies a broader strategy to address various student needs and concerns, fostering a supportive academic environment.

+ GILBANE LAUNCHES LEASING FOR $135M AFFORDABLE HOUSING PROJECT IN D.C.

WASHINGTON, D.C. — Gilbane Development has begun leasing the second of its three-phase redevelopment at 7th Street and Barnaby Road in Southeast Washington, D.C. As of 3rd April 2026, Phase II of Barnaby & 7th includes 229 units within four new residential buildings: The Stoneview, The Solomon, The Marte, and The Lewis.Construction of these buildings is slated for completion by summer 2026. The four communities feature a mix of studio to three-bedroom units serving households earning 50 and 80 percent of the area median income (AMI). Notably, twenty percent of the apartments will be set aside as Permanent Supportive Housing (PSH) units, which will be managed by the district’s Department of Homeless Services.Phase I of Barnaby & 7th consisted of The Upland, which was completed in spring 2025 and includes 169 apartments, with 34 designated as PSH units. The income restrictions for this phase ranged from 30 to 50 percent of AMI. The third phase of development is expected to include approximately 100 additional rental units in one multifamily building, bringing the project to a grand total of 498 units upon completion.During the construction of Phase II, Gilbane also built a new pedestrian walkway designed to connect all six buildings of the larger development. According to the District of Columbia Housing Finance Agency, the total project cost for Phase II was approximately $135 million, underscoring Gilbane's commitment to affordable housing in the region.As the project progresses, it is anticipated to significantly contribute to the availability of affordable housing options in Washington, D.C., while also enhancing community connectivity and providing essential support services for residents in need.

+ CLARK CONSTRUCTION GROUP, LLC [3 News That Matters]

+ CARLY REINER CACERES JOINS CLARK CONSTRUCTION GROUP AS HR DIRECTOR

Carly Reiner Caceres has been appointed as the Director of Human Resources – National Campus Recruiting at Clark Construction Group, bringing a wealth of experience in people operations and talent strategy. On the 29th of April 2026, Caceres officially began her new role, where she will be instrumental in supporting national campus recruiting initiatives aimed at bolstering early-career talent pipelines for one of the largest building and infrastructure companies in the United States.Prior to her appointment at Clark Construction Group, Caceres served as the Vice President of People Operations at Proper, where she was pivotal in shaping the HR strategy and operational support within a hybrid work environment. Her extensive background includes significant roles at MILLER as the Director of Internal Operations and more than nine years at Accenture, where she excelled in various capacities including Talent Strategy Manager and Campus Recruiting Lead.Caceres’ career trajectory showcases her expertise in full-cycle recruiting, stakeholder management, and employer branding, which will be crucial as Clark Construction Group seeks to attract top talent from universities across the nation. Her previous experience managing campus recruiting strategies at Grant Thornton LLP further solidifies her capabilities in building robust internship programs and enhancing student engagement initiatives.Clark Construction Group, founded in 1906, has established a strong reputation for delivering value across major markets, including education and infrastructure. With a commitment to community strengthening and project excellence, the company is poised to benefit from Caceres' strategic insights and leadership in HR, particularly in nurturing the next generation of professionals in the construction industry.Caceres’ early career involved internships and roles with various organizations, including Charleston Magazine and the Smithsonian Institution’s Hirshhorn Museum, which helped her build a diverse skill set in communications and external relations. As she embarks on this new chapter at Clark Construction Group, her multifaceted background is expected to contribute significantly to the company's growth and talent development objectives.

+ CLARK CONTRACTS SECURES MULTI-TRADES FRAMEWORK WITH RENFREWSHIRE COUNCIL

Clark Contracts has been appointed to Renfrewshire Council's multi-trades contractors framework, enhancing the delivery of repair, maintenance, and refurbishment services to domestic properties across the local authority area. This announcement was made on 11th February 2026.The framework, which is set to run until 2027, encompasses a variety of multi-trade works. This will enable Renfrewshire Council to effectively manage both responsive and planned maintenance services across its housing stock, ensuring that local residents receive prompt and reliable service.Clark Contracts is already an established player in the public sector, holding several framework agreements that include Scotland Excel’s New Build Residential Framework and NHS National Services Scotland’s Minor Works Framework. Additionally, the company is involved with the Crown Commercial Service’s Construction Framework and various Scottish Procurement Alliance frameworks focusing on Public Buildings, New Build Housing, and more.Gordon Cunningham, managing director at Clark Contracts, expressed enthusiasm about the new appointment, saying, "Building on our strong track record of delivering more than £200 million in refurbishment projects over the past three years, we are pleased to have been appointed to Renfrewshire Council’s framework. As a Paisley-based business, we are proud to be supporting the council and delivering refurbishment and maintenance services that directly benefit local communities."The addition of this framework is expected to bolster Clark Contracts' already significant presence in the public sector, as the company continues to collaborate with various councils and institutions, including the University of Glasgow and Glasgow City Council, to deliver essential services.

+ CLARK CONSTRUCTION GROUP ADVANCES ₱4-B MIXED-USE HUB AT THE VILLAGES

CLARK FREEPORT – A ₱4-billion mixed-use residential and commercial development in The Villages, a 67-hectare master-planned sports and lifestyle destination here, has been given the green light with the approval by the Clark Development Corp. of an amendment to PKR Corp.’s lease agreement on 13th December 2025. This ambitious project underscores Clark Construction Group's aggressive strategy to attract significant investments and fortify its reputation as Asia’s Leading Meetings and Conference Destination, a title recently bestowed by the World Travel Awards.The revised lease agreement provides PKR Corp. with a 30-month timeline to complete construction and incorporates stringent performance safeguards to ensure timely project delivery. Atty. Agnes VST Devanadera, president and CEO of Clark Development Corp. (CDC), emphasized that the amendment reflects the organization's commitment to fostering investor confidence and efficient execution of projects. "With this agreement, we ensure that it is a good place for everyone to go and with our coordination, we want to be able to maintain that area [The Villages], an area of preference, where people can reside and really be part of the community," Devanadera stated.PKR Corp. president Daekwon Jung expressed gratitude for the partnership with CDC, stating, "We are honored to take part in Clark’s growth and with CDC as our partner, we will deliver this development in Clark." PKR Corp. operates under the umbrella of Kyeryong Construction Industrial Co., Ltd., a South Korean firm known for its expertise in building, civil, residential, and infrastructure projects. The company has received accolades for safety, technology, and workplace practices from various esteemed organizations including the Korea Expressway Corp. and the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy.This mixed-use development is part of CDC’s broader strategy to expedite investments, promote well-planned communities, and establish a business-friendly environment that appeals to both global and local investors. The focus on creating integrated lifestyle hubs aligns with the increasing demand for residential options that complement commercial and recreational spaces, positioning Clark as an attractive destination for future developments.Clark Construction Group's proactive approach in securing this project represents a significant step forward in the region's economic landscape, potentially catalyzing further investments and developments. As the construction timeline unfolds, stakeholders will be keenly observing how this initiative contributes to the overall growth and vibrancy of The Villages and the Clark Freeport Zone.

value.Title: May 01, 2026: VMO2 Ties Long-Term Solar Pact with Egg Power as Part of Net-Zero Drive

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-KSM3-S0RF-V4KG-00000-00May 01, 2026: VMO2 Ties Long-Term Solar Pact with Egg Power as Part of Net-Zero Drive2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:58ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 01, 2026: VMO2 Ties Long-Term Solar Pact with Egg Power as Part of Net-Zero Drive

VMO2 has signed a 10-year PPA with Egg Power to buy energy from a 70MW Suffolk solar farm that is under construction and planned to begin generating in 2027. Announced on 1st May 2026, the deal will supply roughly 5% of VMO2's total energy demand. Egg Power, Liberty Global-owned, complements VMO2's existing wind PPA with The Renewables Infrastructure Group (TRIG) signed last year, creating a broader renewable portfolio.

Mark Hardman, Director of Finance Operations at VMO2, commented, "This agreement with Egg Power is the latest step in Virgin Media O2's journey to achieve net zero emissions by the end of 2040. We’re committed to growing and operating our business in a way that’s good for people and the planet, where we’re cutting carbon, securing renewable energy on a long-term basis, and sourcing renewable energy generation from the UK."

Together, the two PPAs will enable VMO2 to source around 20% of its energy from renewables, reinforcing the company's sustainability drive and its plan to secure long-term, UK-based generation.

INDEX

SECTION 1 SUFFOLK CONSTRUCTION PROFILE

SECTION 2 SUFFOLK CONSTRUCTION TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 3 SUFFOLK CONSTRUCTION RANKING

SECTION 1 SUFFOLK CONSTRUCTION PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Suffolk Construction, founded in 1982 by John Fish in Boston, Massachusetts, is a leading U.S. general contractor. With $5.5 billion in revenue and 2,500 employees, Suffolk delivers complex projects in healthcare, education, and commercial sectors. Notable works include Bostons Millennium Tower and Miamis Brickell City Centre. Employing advanced technologies like BIM and virtual design, Suffolk enhances project efficiency and safety. The company operates 10 offices nationwide, with a strong Northeast presence. Suffolks commitment to sustainability includes LEED-certified projects and eco-friendly practices. Its employee-centric culture, with robust training and diversity initiatives, earned it a spot on Fortunes Best Workplaces list. Suffolk continues to innovate, focusing on smart buildings and urban development.

1.2 SUMMARY

Employees: About 1,150

Fiscal Year End: 31 Aug 2013

Website: http://www.suffolk.com/
